narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 46.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT is 37.27, while successful students typically rank much higher in their Year 1 (average decile 6.04) and Year 3 (average decile 5.91) standings. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, followed by your Year 1 decile, with your LSAT score pulling in the opposite direction. Your lower Year 3 decile ranking stood out as the strongest signal suggesting a higher risk of bar exam struggles, as it places you below the typical performance of those who succeed. Similarly, your Year 1 decile contributed to that concern by indicating early academic challenges compared to passers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was a key positive factor, helping to offset some of those risks and showing your potential strength in that area, much like it does for many admitted students. Your middle family income aligns with about a third of bar passers, and your full-time status matches the vast majority of them, so those didn't sway the decision much. Overall, your profile falls short of the stronger academic deciles and GPA averages seen in accepted applicants, tipping the balance toward rejection. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further or strengthening your recent academic performance to improve those decile rankings—perhaps through targeted coursework or a post-bac program. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas before reapplying. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap; with focused improvements, your chances could shift dramatically in the next cycle.
